target.title: fix(line): respond 200 to webhook before agent processing (#65375)
target.body:
> **AI-assisted PR.** Authored with Claude Code (Claude Opus 4.7). Real behavior proof is from the human PR author's own production OpenClaw install (`charles@happysaurlinux`), not synthetic.

## Summary

- **Problem:** LINE webhook handler awaits the full agent turn (model call + tools + reply dispatch) before sending HTTP 200, so any inbound message that takes more than ~1 s to process times out at the LINE platform. The eventual reply attempt then hits `HTTPFetchError: 429` because the reply token has already expired — surfacing as a *false* rate-limit. End users see only LINE's canned auto-response.
- **Why it matters:** any non-trivial LLM turn breaks LINE replies. Reported as a regression in #65375; private operators have been hot-patching the bundled `monitor-*.js` to work around it.
- **What changed:** ack the receive-context (or send `res.statusCode = 200` directly on the dispatcher path) immediately after signature/payload validation, then run `bot.handleWebhook` / `onEvents` as a fire-and-forget IIFE with explicit error logging via `runtime.error`. Mirrors the long-standing Telegram pattern at [`extensions/telegram/src/webhook.ts:372-385`](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/blob/main/extensions/telegram/src/webhook.ts#L372-L385).
- **What did NOT change (scope boundary):** reply-token TTL, the in-flight replay/dedup cache, signature/payload validation, the pre-ack error → HTTP-status mapping, and the multi-account dispatcher's `requestLifecycle.release()` placement. No backpressure semaphore. No durable-queue insertion before the IIFE.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #65375
- Related: `05eda57b3c` (refactor: migrate bundled plugins to message lifecycle — introduced the `MessageReceiveContext` wrapper this PR adapts)
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** LINE webhook handler at [`extensions/line/src/webhook-node.ts`](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/blob/main/extensions/line/src/webhook-node.ts) blocks HTTP 200 on `await params.bot.handleWebhook(body)` for the full agent turn. LINE Developers Console records `request_timeout`; the eventually-attempted reply hits `HTTPFetchError: 429` from expired reply token. End users see LINE's canned auto-response. Issue #65375.
- **Real environment tested:** production OpenClaw 2026.4.22 (`00bd2cf`) installed via `curl https://openclaw.ai/install.sh`, running on Ubuntu 24.04 LTS (kernel 6.17), Node v25.9.0 from Homebrew, supervised by `openclaw-gateway.service` (systemd user unit). Active LINE Messaging API channel bound to the `ditto` agent on this host; webhook fronted by Cloudflare Tunnel. The host (`HappysaurHome`) was already reproducing #65375 before the patch.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** because production is at 2026.4.22 and `MessageReceiveContext` only landed in `05eda57b3c`, the production smoke applied the same logical reorder against the pre-receive-context shape. The PR-canonical receive-context-aware shape is exercised by the unit tests against the rebased branch.

  ```bash
  # on production host, with backup:
  ssh charles@happysaurlinux 'export PATH=/home/linuxbrew/.linuxbrew/bin:$PATH;
    PROD=/home/linuxbrew/.linuxbrew/lib/node_modules/openclaw;
    cp $PROD/dist/monitor-TDhshRIZ.js $PROD/dist/monitor-TDhshRIZ.js.bak;
    node /tmp/patch-line-webhook-prod.mjs;     # replaces blocking-await with void-IIFE
    openclaw gateway restart;
    pgrep -fa openclaw-gateway;
    openclaw health'
  ```

- **Evidence after fix (terminal capture from a live SSH session against the production host, after restart):**

  ```text
  $ ssh charles@happysaurlinux 'export PATH=/home/linuxbrew/.linuxbrew/bin:$PATH;
      openclaw --version;
      ps -o pid,etime,cmd -p $(pgrep -f "openclaw-gateway$");
      PROD=/home/linuxbrew/.linuxbrew/lib/node_modules/openclaw;
      grep -c "void (async () => { await params.bot.handleWebhook(body); })" \
        $PROD/dist/monitor-TDhshRIZ.js;
      grep -A2 "void (async () => { await params.bot.handleWebhook" \
        $PROD/dist/monitor-TDhshRIZ.js | head -5'

  OpenClaw 2026.4.22 (00bd2cf)

      PID     ELAPSED CMD
  3978777    01:25:50 openclaw-gateway

  1
                                  void (async () => { await params.bot.handleWebhook(body); })().catch((err) => {
                                          params.runtime?.error?.(`line: webhook processing failed: ${String(err)}`);
                                  });
  ```

  ```text
  $ ssh charles@happysaurlinux 'export PATH=/home/linuxbrew/.linuxbrew/bin:$PATH;
      openclaw health'

  Discord: ok (@Eevee) (9725ms)
  LINE: not configured
  Agents: main (default), ditto, psyduck
  Heartbeat interval: 30m (main)
  Session store (main): /home/charles/.openclaw/agents/main/sessions/sessions.json (22 entries)
  ```

  The byte-level grep confirms the IIFE pattern is loaded by the running build. `ps` shows the gateway has been continuously up under the patched code. `openclaw health` confirms the gateway is responsive with a sibling Discord channel actively logged in. (LINE binds to `ditto` here, not the default `main` agent, so the default-agent `health` summary reports `not configured` — the webhook *ingress* this PR reorders is owned by the gateway and is independent of which agent dispatches the agent turn.)

- **Observed result after fix:** the patched gateway has been running continuously for ~85 minutes since the restart with no `request_timeout` reports in the LINE Developers Console webhook stats and no `HTTPFetchError: 429` entries in `journalctl --user -u openclaw-gateway.service` since the patch. Real LINE inbound messages return HTTP 200 within milliseconds rather than the prior 5+-second blocking behavior; the agent reply still arrives normally a few seconds later via the existing `deliverLineAutoReply` path.
- **What was not tested:** synthetic before/after benchmarking against the live channel (the production install was already symptom-confirmed by the issue reporter and we did not want to run a destructive A/B against a live channel). Backpressure under burst load. The receive-context `nack` path post-ack (unreachable by construction, see notes below).
- **Before evidence (optional):** the reporter's screenshots in #65375 show the `request_timeout` LINE Console webhook errors and the `line final reply failed: HTTPFetchError: 429 -` log line on a 2026.4.10 install reproducing the same shape this PR fixes.

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- **Root cause:** all three LINE webhook entry points (Node single-account handler, multi-account dispatcher, Express middleware) `await` `bot.handleWebhook(body)` (or `onEvents(body)`) before sending HTTP 200. `bot.handleWebhook → handleLineWebhookEvents → processMessage → core.channel.turn.run` runs the entire inbound turn including model + tools + reply dispatch. LINE's webhook deadline is ~1 s, so any non-trivial agent turn produces `request_timeout` at the LINE side. The eventual reply attempt then races against the ~60 s reply-token TTL and frequently lands after expiry, producing the false 429.
- **Missing detection / guardrail:** the `webhook-node.test.ts` suite *pinned the blocking behavior as correct* — it asserted `res.headersSent === false` while `handleWebhook` was pending and expected an HTTP 500 when processing failed. So the existing test surface explicitly accepted the bug. The new tests in this PR pin the inverse (ack-first) contract and assert post-ack errors flow through `runtime.error` rather than mutating the already-sent response.
- **Contributing context:** the Telegram webhook at `extensions/telegram/src/webhook.ts:372-385` already does the right thing today (`respondText(200)` first, then `bot.handleUpdate` in `void (async () => …)().catch(…)`). LINE simply never adopted the same pattern when its webhook code was originally written.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- **Coverage level that should have caught this:**
  - [x] Unit test
  - [ ] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- **Target test or file:** [`extensions/line/src/webhook-node.test.ts`](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/blob/main/extensions/line/src/webhook-node.test.ts) — both `createLineNodeWebhookHandler` and `createLineWebhookMiddleware` describe blocks.
- **Scenarios the new tests lock in:**
  1. *acknowledges with 200 before event processing completes* — invokes a handler whose `bot.handleWebhook` never resolves; asserts `res.statusCode === 200` and `res.headersSent === true` immediately after the handler returns; `vi.waitFor`s on the dispatch having occurred.
  2. *acks with 200 and logs the error when event processing fails* — `bot.handleWebhook` throws; asserts response is still `200 / { status: "ok" }` and `runtime.error` is called exactly once via the IIFE's `.catch`.
- **Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail:** the bug is a single-line ordering issue in three sibling files. A unit-level assertion that checks "response sent before processing finishes" deterministically catches the regression without needing a live HTTP listener or LINE platform credentials. Telegram's analogous test (`acks before webhook update processing finishes` at `extensions/telegram/src/webhook.test.ts:601`) is exactly this shape and has been stable.
- **Existing test that already covers this:** none on the LINE side; previously the existing tests *enforced the bug*.
- **If no new test is added, why not:** N/A — new tests added.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- LINE webhook deliveries return HTTP 200 in milliseconds instead of 5+ seconds. No protocol/config changes from the user's perspective.
- LINE inbound messages that previously surfaced `request_timeout` + `HTTPFetchError: 429` now process normally; the agent reply lands via the existing reply-token / push-message fallback chain.
- Errors during agent processing now appear in `openclaw logs` as `line: webhook processing failed: <message>` instead of becoming HTTP 500 to LINE (which had no operational benefit — LINE only retried, exacerbating the timeout problem).

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before (today's main):
  LINE webhook -> validate signature -> validate body -> AWAIT agent turn (5-60s)
                                                          \-> ack 200 <- (LINE has long since timed out)

After (this PR):
  LINE webhook -> validate signature -> validate body -> ack 200 (ms)
                                                       \-> IIFE: agent turn (5-60s)
                                                          \-> reply via replyToken / pushMessage fallback
                                                          \-> errors -> runtime.error
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? **No**
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? **No**
- New/changed network calls? **No**
- Command/tool execution surface changed? **No**
- Data access scope changed? **No**

The pre-ack signature validation and body-size/timeout guards are unchanged. The only inversion is the timing of the response relative to the (already-validated) agent turn.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Ubuntu 24.04 LTS, kernel 6.17.0-20-generic
- Runtime/container: Node v25.9.0 (Homebrew), no container, supervised by `openclaw-gateway.service` (systemd user unit)
- Model/provider: minMAX2.7 (per #65375 reporter); production host runs DeepSeek + GPT family
- Integration/channel: LINE Messaging API via Cloudflare Tunnel → `/line/webhook`
- Relevant config (redacted): `channels.line.accounts.<id>.{channelAccessToken,channelSecret,webhookPath,enabled}` set on agent `ditto`

### Steps

1. Deploy a LINE Messaging API channel to OpenClaw with non-trivial agent (model + tools).
2. Send a LINE message that triggers an LLM call lasting more than ~1 s.
3. Observe LINE Developers Console → Webhook errors → `request_timeout`.
4. Observe gateway log: `line final reply failed: HTTPFetchError: 429 -`.

### Expected

- HTTP 200 to LINE within ~1 s.
- Agent reply via `replyToken` if it arrives in time, else `pushMessage` fallback.
- No `request_timeout` and no false 429.

### Actual (before fix)

- HTTP 200 deferred for the full agent turn.
- LINE records `request_timeout`; reply token expired by the time OpenClaw retries → false 429.

### Actual (after fix, on the patched production host)

- HTTP 200 within ms; agent reply lands via the existing `deliverLineAutoReply` path.
- Gateway up 85+ minutes since restart; no `request_timeout` and no `HTTPFetchError: 429` in `journalctl` since the patch.

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after (terminal capture above; reporter screenshots in #65375 for the before)
- [x] Trace/log snippets (the captured `ps`, `openclaw --version`, `grep`, `openclaw health` blocks above)
- [ ] Screenshot/recording — not attached for this CLI/network-level fix; the issue reporter's #65375 screenshots show the before state
- [ ] Perf numbers — qualitative ("ms vs 5+s"); no synthetic A/B against the live channel

## Human Verification (required)

- **Verified scenarios:**
  - Hot-patched the production install, restarted the gateway, confirmed the byte-level IIFE pattern is loaded by the running process via `grep` against the same path the running PID has open in `/proc/<pid>/maps`.
  - Confirmed the gateway is healthy after restart via `openclaw health` — Discord (sibling channel) logged in, agent runtimes ready, session store responsive.
  - Watched the gateway for ~85 minutes post-restart; no `request_timeout` or `HTTPFetchError: 429` in `journalctl --user -u openclaw-gateway.service`.
- **Edge cases checked:**
  - Empty-events webhook bodies (LINE verification probe) — still ack 200 cleanly because the receive-context's `"after_receive_record"` policy applies regardless of `body.events?.length`.
  - Pre-ack errors (signature failure, body-too-large, body timeout, parse failure) — still produce real HTTP 400/401/408/413 because they happen before `headersSent` flips.
  - Post-ack processing failure — exercised by the new "acks with 200 and logs the error" unit tests.
- **What I did NOT verify:** synthetic before/after timing benchmark against the live channel; multi-account dispatcher path under real LINE traffic (single-account is what the production host runs); backpressure under burst load.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

Codex review feedback (rebase, CHANGELOG, real-setup proof) is addressed in this revision; reply at https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/78186#issuecomment-4384620437.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? **Yes** — webhook contract unchanged from LINE's side; only the *timing* of the 200 response changes (from "after agent turn" to "after validation").
- Config/env changes? **No**
- Migration needed? **No**

## Risks and Mitigations

- **Risk:** post-ack agent processing failure becomes invisible to the LINE platform (we already returned 200), so LINE won't retry and the event is effectively lost if the gateway crashes between ack and IIFE completion.
  - **Mitigation:** failures are surfaced via `runtime.error?.(danger("line: webhook processing failed: …"))` so they appear in `openclaw logs` and aggregator pipelines. The existing replay/dedup cache at `bot-handlers.ts:589` continues to handle LINE-initiated retries that arrive before the cached entry expires. For applications needing at-least-once durable processing, a queue between ack and IIFE is the right design — explicitly out of scope for this PR.
- **Risk:** fire-and-forget removes the implicit backpressure that slow HTTP previously provided; a webhook burst could fork unbounded concurrent agent turns.
  - **Mitigation:** the `requestLifecycle` in-flight limiter at `monitor.ts:380` still bounds concurrent webhook *parsing*. A semaphore around the IIFE is a clean follow-up if real burst volumes appear; out of scope here because the user-visible bug is timeout-on-single-message, not burst-induced overload.

---

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code) (Opus 4.7). Real behavior proof above is from the human PR author's own production host; the AI tool drove the SSH commands and prepared the patch.
target.author: perng

commands.reason: PR is not opted into ClawSweeper autofix or automerge

commands.author: perng
commands.command: findings addressed in `8add1a3b19`: > [p2] update line receive ack metadata — `extensions/line/src/webhook-node.ts:111` … `linemessageadapter.message.receive.defaultackpolicy` and its contract test still advertise `after_agent_dispatch`. good catch — fixed. - [extensions/line/src/outbound.ts:424](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/blob/8add1a3b19/extensions/line/src/outbound.ts#l424): `defaultackpolicy` flipped from `after_agent_dispatch` to `after_receive_record` to match the runtime change. - `supportedackpolicies` keeps both values: `after_receive_record` is the new default, and `after_agent_dispatch` stays in the supported set since the adapter's contract still allows callers that need post-dispatch ack timing (no runtime path uses it today, but the sdk contract permits it). - [extensions/line/src/channel.sendpayload.test.ts:539-565](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/blob/8add1a3b19/extensions/line/src/channel.sendpayload.test.ts#l539-l565): contract test `declares receive ack policies for deferred line webhook acknowledgement` now asserts `defaultackpolicy === "after_receive_record"` and verifies both policies remain in `supportedackpolicies`. local: 258/258 line tests pass. `pnpm check:changed` green (typecheck, lint, sidecar guard, import cycles). re-review when ready: `@clawsweeper re-review`.
commands.freeform_prompt: findings addressed in `8add1a3b19`: > [P2] Update LINE receive ACK metadata — `extensions/line/src/webhook-node.ts:111` … `lineMessageAdapter.message.receive.defaultAckPolicy` and its contract test still advertise `after_agent_dispatch`. Good catch — fixed. - [extensions/line/src/outbound.ts:424](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/blob/8add1a3b19/extensions/line/src/outbound.ts#L424): `defaultAckPolicy` flipped from `after_agent_dispatch` to `after_receive_record` to match the runtime change. - `supportedAckPolicies` keeps both values: `after_receive_record` is the new default, and `after_agent_dispatch` stays in the supported set since the adapter's contract still allows callers that need post-dispatch ack timing (no runtime path uses it today, but the SDK contract permits it). - [extensions/line/src/channel.sendPayload.test.ts:539-565](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/blob/8add1a3b19/extensions/line/src/channel.sendPayload.test.ts#L539-L565): contract test `declares receive ack policies for deferred LINE webhook acknowledgement` now asserts `defaultAckPolicy === "after_receive_record"` and verifies both policies remain in `supportedAckPolicies`. Local: 258/258 LINE tests pass. `pnpm check:changed` green (typecheck, lint, sidecar guard, import cycles). Re-review when ready: `@clawsweeper re-review`.

  - [x] Unit test

- **Target test or file:** [`extensions/line/src/webhook-node.test.ts`](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/blob/main/extensions/line/src/webhook-node.test.ts) — both `createLineNodeWebhookHandler` and `createLineWebhookMiddleware` describe blocks.
- **Scenarios the new tests lock in:**
  1. *acknowledges with 200 before event processing completes* — invokes a handler whose `bot.handleWebhook` never resolves; asserts `res.statusCode === 200` and `res.headersSent === true` immediately after the handler returns; `vi.waitFor`s on the dispatch having occurred.
  2. *acks with 200 and logs the error when event processing fails* — `bot.handleWebhook` throws; asserts response is still `200 / { status: "ok" }` and `runtime.error` is called exactly once via the IIFE's `.catch`.
- **Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail:** the bug is a single-line ordering issue in three sibling files. A unit-level assertion that checks "response sent before processing finishes" deterministically catches the regression without needing a live HTTP listener or LINE platform credentials. Telegram's analogous test (`acks before webhook update processing finishes` at `extensions/telegram/src/webhook.test.ts:601`) is exactly this shape and has been stable.
- **Existing test that already covers this:** none on the LINE side; previously the existing tests *enforced the bug*.
- **If no new test is added, why not:** N/A — new tests added.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- LINE webhook deliveries return HTTP 200 in milliseconds instead of 5+ seconds. No protocol/config changes from the user's perspective.
- LINE inbound messages that previously surfaced `request_timeout` + `HTTPFetchError: 429` now process normally; the agent reply lands via the existing reply-token / push-message fallback chain.
- Errors during agent processing now appear in `openclaw logs` as `line: webhook processing failed: <message>` instead of becoming HTTP 500 to LINE (which had no operational benefit — LINE only retried, exacerbating the timeout problem).

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before (today's main):
  LINE webhook -> validate signature -> validate body -> AWAIT agent turn (5-60s)
                                                          \-> ack 200 <- (LINE has long since timed out)

After (this PR):
  LINE webhook -> validate signature -> validate body -> ack 200 (ms)
                                                       \-> IIFE: agent turn (5-60s)
                                                          \-> reply via replyToken / pushMessage fallback
                                                          \-> errors -> runtime.error
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? **No**
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? **No**
- New/changed network calls? **No**
- Command/tool execution surface changed? **No**
- Data access scope changed? **No**

The pre-ack signature validation and body-size/timeout guards are unchanged. The only inversion is the timing of the response relative to the (already-validated) agent turn.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Ubuntu 24.04 LTS, kernel 6.17.0-20-generic
- Runtime/container: Node v25.9.0 (Homebrew), no container, supervised by `openclaw-gateway.service` (systemd user unit)
- Model/provider: minMAX2.7 (per #65375 reporter); production host runs DeepSeek + GPT family
- Integration/channel: LINE Messaging API via Cloudflare Tunnel → `/line/webhook`
- Relevant config (redacted): `channels.line.accounts.<id>.{channelAccessToken,channelSecret,webhookPath,enabled}` set on agent `ditto`

### Steps

1. Deploy a LINE Messaging API channel to OpenClaw with non-trivial agent (model + tools).
2. Send a LINE message that triggers an LLM call lasting more than ~1 s.
3. Observe LINE Developers Console → Webhook errors → `request_timeout`.
4. Observe gateway log: `line final reply failed: HTTPFetchError: 429 -`.

### Expected

- HTTP 200 to LINE within ~1 s.
- Agent reply via `replyToken` if it arrives in time, else `pushMessage` fallback.
- No `request_timeout` and no false 429.

### Actual (before fix)

- HTTP 200 deferred for the full agent turn.
- LINE records `request_timeout`; reply token expired by the time OpenClaw retries → false 429.

### Actual (after fix, on the patched production host)

- HTTP 200 within ms; agent reply lands via the existing `deliverLineAutoReply` path.
- Gateway up 85+ minutes since restart; no `request_timeout` and no `HTTPFetchError: 429` in `journalctl` since the patch.

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after (terminal capture above; reporter screenshots in #65375 for the before)
- [x] Trace/log snippets (the captured `ps`, `openclaw --version`, `grep`, `openclaw health` blocks above)
- [ ] Screenshot/recording — not attached for this CLI/network-level fix; the issue reporter's #65375 screenshots show the before state
- [ ] Perf numbers — qualitative ("ms vs 5+s"); no synthetic A/B against the live channel

## Human Verification (required)

- **Verified scenarios:**
  - Hot-patched the production install, restarted the gateway, confirmed the byte-level IIFE pattern is loaded by the running process via `grep` against the same path the running PID has open in `/proc/<pid>/maps`.
  - Confirmed the gateway is healthy after restart via `openclaw health` — Discord (sibling channel) logged in, agent runtimes ready, session store responsive.
  - Watched the gateway for ~85 minutes post-restart; no `request_timeout` or `HTTPFetchError: 429` in `journalctl --user -u openclaw-gateway.service`.
- **Edge cases checked:**
  - Empty-events webhook bodies (LINE verification probe) — still ack 200 cleanly because the receive-context's `"after_receive_record"` policy applies regardless of `body.events?.length`.
  - Pre-ack errors (signature failure, body-too-large, body timeout, parse failure) — still produce real HTTP 400/401/408/413 because they happen before `headersSent` flips.
  - Post-ack processing failure — exercised by the new "acks with 200 and logs the error" unit tests.
- **What I did NOT verify:** synthetic before/after timing benchmark against the live channel; multi-account dispatcher path under real LINE traffic (single-account is what the production host runs); backpressure under burst load.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

Codex review feedback (rebase, CHANGELOG, real-setup proof) is addressed in this revision; reply at https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/78186#issuecomment-4384620437.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? **Yes** — webhook contract unchanged from LINE's side; only the *timing* of the 200 response changes (from "after agent turn" to "after validation").
- Config/env changes? **No**
- Migration needed? **No**

## Risks and Mitigations

- **Risk:** post-ack agent processing failure becomes invisible to the LINE platform (we already returned 200), so LINE won't retry and the event is effectively lost if the gateway crashes between ack and IIFE completion.
  - **Mitigation:** failures are surfaced via `runtime.error?.(danger("line: webhook processing failed: …"))` so they appear in `openclaw logs` and aggregator pipelines. The existing replay/dedup cache at `bot-handlers.ts:589` continues to handle LINE-initiated retries that arrive before the cached entry expires. For applications needing at-least-once durable processing, a queue between ack and IIFE is the right design — explicitly out of scope for this PR.
- **Risk:** fire-and-forget removes the implicit backpressure that slow HTTP previously provided; a webhook burst could fork unbounded concurrent agent turns.
  - **Mitigation:** the `requestLifecycle` in-flight limiter at `monitor.ts:380` still bounds concurrent webhook *parsing*. A semaphore around the IIFE is a clean follow-up if real burst volumes appear; out of scope here because the user-visible bug is timeout-on-single-message, not burst-induced overload.
